EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes brought 4 to 8 inches of snow across Knox and coastal Waldo Counties.  In general, 1 to 5 inches of snow fell across the remainder of western Maine.

Low pressure intensifying across the maritime provinces drew arctic air across the region. Winds between the intense slow moving maritimes low and arctic high pressure across Ontario province remained strong enough to combine with sub-zero temperatures to produce an extended interval of dangerous wind chills. The winds combined with sub-zero temperatures to frequently produce wind chills of 30 to 50 below zero.

Low pressure intensifying across the maritime provinces drew arctic air across the region. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph...with gusts to 40 mph at times...developed between the intense maritimes low and arctic high pressure to the west drawing the arctic air across the region and producing dangerous wind chills. The winds combined with low temperatures of 15 to 30 below zero across northern areas and lows of 10 to 20 below zero Downeast to frequently produce wind chills of 45 to 65 below zero across northern areas with wind chills of 35 to 55 below zero Downeast. Temperatures remained below zero across northern areas during this time. High temperatures on the 15th only reached 10 to 15 below zero across northern areas...barely reaching zero along the Downeast coast. The extreme wind chills prompted many schools to close and resulted in the cancellation of events...particularly across northern portions of the region.

Low pressure moved northeast through the Gulf of Maine during the morning hours of December 11th and caused coastal flooding in Saco.  Gale force winds accompanied the storm.  Surf Street in Saco flooded and about 175 feet of roadway was undermined, causing approximately $100,000 in damage.  In Wells and Kennebunk, the surf threw rocks onto coastal roads.

Strong winds downed trees onto power lines causing numerous power outages throughout the area.  According to Central Maine Power Company, about 11,000 customers lost power in southern Maine.  In Lebanon, a wind gust toppled a large pine tree crushing part of a home.

A coastal low developed near the outer banks of North Carolina on the morning of the 28th and moved northeast spreading heavy rain over northern New England.   In general, 1 to 3 inches of rain fell across the area with the heaviest falling in the Upper Androscoggin and Kennebec River Valleys.  Minor flooding occurred along several small streams in Oxford and Franklin Counties late in the evening of the 28th.  The Swift River in Roxbury crested at 8.4 feet at 2:15 am on the 29th.  Flood stage is 7 feet.  The Kennebec River crested just under flood stage.

Low pressure developed in southwest Pennsylvania on the morning of December 23rd and moved northeast through western New York spreading rain into Maine. Rainfall Amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common with Andover receiving 2.04 inches. This was enough rain to bring small rivers and streams over their banks resulting in minor flooding.  The Swift River in Roxbury was above the 7 foot flood stage from 10:45 pm on the 23rd until 5:15 am on the 24th, reaching a crest of 8.48 feet at 11:30 pm on the 23rd.

Low pressure tracked from the Great Lakes toward New England during the 21st while a secondary low developed and lifted northeast along the New England coast. Snow began across the region during the morning of the 21st. The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon then continued...heavy at times...into the morning of the 22nd. The low exited east of Nova Scotia during the afternoon of the 22nd...bringing the snow to an end. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 11 inches. Numerous accidents were attributed to snow covered and slippery roads.

Low pressure intensified as it moved into the Gulf of Maine on the night of Saturday, February 21, spreading snow northward into Central and Northern Maine. Snow tapered to flurries on Sunday morning, February 22, as the storm moved into Eastern Canada. Snowfall amounts ranged from near nothing in southwestern coastal areas to 5 to 9 inches in the Western Maine mountains to 6 to 13 inches in Central and Mid Coast Maine.

Low pressure over the Great Lakes redeveloped off the mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, February 5, and tracked northeast along a stationary front Friday night and into the Gulf of Maine by early Saturday morning February 6. Snow began spreading into Southern New Hampshire and Maine on Friday morning. Snow changed to sleet and freezing rain in coastal and southern interior section of Maine and New Hampshire where accumulations by Saturday morning measured 1 to 3 inches.  Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were recorded over the mountains and foothills.

A slow moving cutoff low pressure system produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in extreme Southwest Maine. Rainfall began on Thursday, April 1, and continued through the morning of Friday, April 2. Many small rivers and streams county wide experienced flooding.

Lightning struck a house in North Belgrade and caused extensive damage throughout the home. The lightning reportedly struck a tree in the backyard, traveled into the ground through the trees roots, through the family pool and into the home. In the home, the lightning damaged the foundation, collapsed a wall, shattered glass, damaged the home's entire electrical system, and destroyed equipment plugged into the electrical outlets. Repairs were expected to take up to 4 months to complete.

Strong winds developed across the region in the wake of a cold front. The winds snapped branches and toppled many trees and power lines...contributing to power outages. Low pressure developing off the mid Atlantic coast during the early morning hours of Tuesday, February 3, moved northeast through the day, reaching the New England coastal waters Tuesday night. Snow moved into southern New England Tuesday evening and overspread New Hampshire and Western Maine overnight.  Snow accumulated 1 to 3 inches across interior sections of Southern Maine and New Hampshire where precipitation mixed with sleet and freezing rain. Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches in the mountains and foothills.

Low pressure over the Great Lakes redeveloped off the mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, February 5, and tracked northeast along a stationary front Friday night and into the Gulf of Maine by early Saturday morning February 6. Snow began spreading into Southern New Hampshire and Maine on Friday morning. Snow changed to sleet and freezing rain in coastal and southern interior section of Maine and New Hampshire where accumulations by Saturday morning measured 1 to 3 inches.  Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were recorded over the mountains and foothills.

Severe thunderstorms moving through Cumberland County on the afternoon of June 3, 2004, caused wind damage in Westbrook Maine at approximately 255 pm. The public reported trees down on cars on Seaby Street in Westbrook.

Severe thunderstorms moving through Western Maine on the afternoon of June 9, 2004 knocked down wires in Fryeburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation of  3 to 6 inches was common across the Saint John Valley...with locally higher totals. Potato fields along the Saint John River were flooded. Also...a mudslide near Saint Francis eroded a portion of Route 161...narrowing the road to one lane. Several culverts were also washed out in the Saint John Valley.

Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches. Several rural roads were flooded or washed out...stranding some residents overnight.

Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the region...filling many brooks to capacity and causing some to overflow their banks.  The overflowing waters covered or washed out portions of some roads.
Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a transmitter tower building for the Waldo County Regional Communications Center causing an estimated $4000 in damage.  The lightning strike and resulting fire destroyed a 12 kilowatt generator, a block heater, and a steel door.

Lightning cause a fire that destroyed a home in Searsmont.  The lightning struck a tree 30 to 40 feet away from the home and reportedly followed the tree's roots to a grounding rod for the home's electrical system.  The lightning followed the electrical system inside the home where it ignited the fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced several inches of rain in a short time. The heavy rains caused the Caribou Stream to overflow its banks and wash out a portion of Bonderson Road.

Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation in the Millinocket area generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches. The heavy rains contributed to road closures near the Millinocket Stream due to ponding from elevated water levels.

Moisture associated with the remnants of  Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation in the vicinity of Baxter State Park generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with localized 8 to 10 inch totals in orographically favored areas.  Travel was restricted across southern portions of the park due to dangerous water crossings and flooded roads.

Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bonnie brought heavy rains to the region. Storm total precipitation of 4 to 5 inches was reported in the Whiting area. The heavy rains washed out portions of a private road near Whiting.
The winds partially tore the roof from a mobile home. Several trees were also blown down.
The winds tore the roof from a mobile home...with the roof then blown onto a vehicle and barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire in a home in Wayne causing minor damage.

Training thunderstorms with very heavy rains produced up to 4 inches of rain in 1 to 2 hours. Several private roads in rural Northern Somerset county were flooded and not passable 35 miles north of Jackman.
Downburst winds associated with a strong thunderstorm at the south end of Long Lake resulted in numerous trees down and damage to buildings and vehicles in Naples.

Lightning struck a home in Bethel causing an estimated $2000 in damage.  The lightning cracked a wall in the home and traveled through the home's electrical system, destroying a television, computer, and garage door opener.
A 50-yr-old man was struck an injured by lightning as he walked outside his home after the storm had passed.  He was taken to a nearby hospital and released 8 hours later.

Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported across the region. The strongest winds generally occurred across the higher terrain of the region and Downeast. Nearly 7,000 customers lost power across Penobscot...Piscataquis and Hancock counties.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Gulf of Maine through New Brunswick brought the first significant snow of the season to the region. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with localized totals of around 12 inches. The heavy wet snow produced hazardous driving conditions...contributing to many accidents. The heavy wet snow also brought down some power lines leading to scattered power outages.

Intensifying low pressure tracking northeast toward Nova Scotia brought heavy snow and gusty winds to portions of Downeast Maine. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches across Hancock county. The heaviest snow fell across the southeast corner of Washington county where storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals to 15 inches. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph...with occasional gusts in excess of 40 mph...produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with near blizzard conditions at times.
